Based on the same book, this film changed the rules of horror cinema for the better and, in the process, propelled Anthony Hopkins and Jodie Foster to stardom. The suspense is steady throughout its nearly two hours and remains compelling no matter how many times you watch it.

One of the best movies of the 20th century. Thanks largely to the magnetic performances of Sir Anthony Hopkins, Jodie Foster, Scott Glenn, Ted Levine, and Jonathan Demme’s superb directing, The Silence of the Lambs has become immortalized as a film you can watch over and over again without getting bored. Everything succeeds here. The acting, screenplay, themes, and plot are so compelling that they keep the audience captivated from start to finish. What makes the film especially powerful is not only Clarice Starling’s internal struggle to prove herself and confront her demons, but also the classic good-versus-evil conflict full of twists and turns. Demme’s use of POV shots through Starling’s perspective is especially effective. Five stars all day, and I highly recommend seeing it if you have not already.

This is a rare perfect film. This transcends the typical horror film with the director's constant use of tight, direct-to-camera close-ups which forces an uncomfortable intimacy with the characters. While Anthony Hopkins' portrayal of Hannibal Lecter is the quintessential magnetic, high-functioning nightmare that dominates the zeitgeist, it is Jodie Foster's grounded, vulnerable portrayal that provides the character exploration of the suffocating world that Clarice inhabits. Plus in the haunting world where (alot, not all) transgenders commit horrible acts of violence, this film gives a realistic portrayal into their disturbing mindset. It’s atmospheric, highly intelligent, and relentlessly tense—a masterpiece for a reason. 5/5 10/10

I really like the make-up in this movie :D It's really convincing, and pratical effects don't get old like CGI does. That's why The Birds by Hitchcock is nearly unwatchable while this movie still delievers chills three decades later. It's honestely one of if not THE scariest movie I've watched. I don't really know I'm saying that like it's a huge millestone or something, but anyways, I also like the story and the direction SO MUCH. Please do yourself a favour and watch it if you haven't already!

I have always admired this film, not least for the two excellent leads in Foster and Hopkins. But for the first time ever I got round to watching "Manhunter" yesterday, and I have to say I found that film far more compelling and rounded compared to Lambs. The protagonists in that film - Peterson and Cox - were equally outstanding as Hopkins and Foster were here, but the film overall had a far tighter direction and an intensity that was a little lacking in Lambs, Moreover the final 10 minutes and the confrontation between Foster and BB just felt a little forced and unrealistic in all honesty. That said, Lambs is a great film. But had it not being the towering performance of Hopkins this could have been just another routine thriller/horror film

A suspenseful and disturbing film that captures the psychological mindset of cold-blooded murderers, particularly the tension between speech, skin, and their respective (in)efficacy. Both Buffalo Bill and Lecter mirror Clarice Starling’s psychological struggle, which unfolds between accommodating a masculinist firmness and inhabiting a feminine body continually framed as an object of sexual desire within her work environment. Anthony Hopkins elevates the film through the tension he conveys on screen. His demeanor and confidence, consistently outwitting his interlocutors, reflect the intellectual arrogance he craves amid the perversity surrounding the victims. Jodie Foster, embodying a frail yet rebellious presence in a highly masculinist setting, provides a strong contrast, though her performance never reaches the level of her adversary. While the repetitive close-ups reinforce the film’s confined and schizophrenic mood, Foster's frequent facial expressions become unrewarding

The Silence of the Lambs (1991) is an extraordinary psychological thriller that combines suspense, intelligence, and unforgettable performances into one of the most haunting films ever made. Directed by Jonathan Demme and based on Thomas Harris’s novel, it’s a masterpiece of tension and character — a film that both terrifies and fascinates in equal measure. Jodie Foster delivers a career-defining performance as Clarice Starling, a young FBI trainee determined to stop a serial killer known as “Buffalo Bill.” To do so, she must seek the help of the brilliant but terrifying Dr. Hannibal Lecter, a cultured psychiatrist — and cannibal — played with chilling precision by Anthony Hopkins. The chemistry between Foster and Hopkins is electric. Their scenes together are charged with psychological intensity, blending fear, curiosity, and mutual respect. Hopkins’s portrayal of Lecter is legendary — sophisticated, eerily calm, and utterly unnerving — earning him a well-deserved Academy Award for Best Actor. Foster’s performance is equally powerful, combining vulnerability and courage in a way that makes Clarice one of the most compelling heroines in modern cinema. Jonathan Demme’s direction is masterful. The film’s pacing is deliberate, its atmosphere taut with suspense. The use of close-up shots creates a sense of intimacy and unease, drawing the viewer into the psychological duel between hunter and hunted. The cinematography, score, and sound design all work together to heighten the tension without relying on excessive violence or cheap scares. What sets The Silence of the Lambs apart is its depth. Beneath the horror lies a story about power, control, and the courage to face inner demons. Clarice’s journey is as much psychological as it is procedural — a battle not just against evil, but against fear itself.

Going into this movie, I wasn't expecting much. I was anticipating an overlong episode of Criminal Minds. After finishing this movie, my mind was completely changed. Simply put, The Silence of the Lambs deserves all the praise that it received. Jodie Foster is incredible, Anthony Hopkins is chilling, the filmography is crisp, the pacing is brilliant. Honestly, I cannot think of a single negative thing to say about it. I was on the edge of my seat the whole time and oftentimes peeking from behind my covered eyes. Best Character: Hannibal Lecter Best Quote: "Well, Clarice - have the lambs stopped screaming?" - Hannibal Best Scene: Every Meeting between Clarice and Hannibal Best Piece of Score: "Clarice" Note: The final line of "I'm having an old friend for dinner." might be the best final line in the history of cinema.
